coredata.dc:title: Shape-engineered carbon quantum dots embedded on CdS-nanorods for enhanced visible light harvesting towards photocatalytic application
coredata.dc:creator: Moniruzzaman, Md
Kim, Jongsung
coredata.dc:description: Shape-engineered yellow-emissive carbon quantum dots (Y-CQDs) with discernible edges were synthesized. Three-fold-symmetric benzene-1,3,5-triol was a suitable precursor for controlling the specific structure and shape of CQDs via dehydration-facilitated covalent polymerization in sulfuric acid within 90 min of a thermal reaction at 190 °C. The formation mechanism involves ring cyclization of benzene-1,3,5-triol via a six-membered fashion through elimination of the neighboring active -H and –OH groups. Binary heterostructure nanocomposites, comprising CdS-nanorods (NRs) and Y-CQDs, were prepared using simple sonication followed by solvent evaporation under stirring. The nanocomposite with 6 wt% YCQDs (CdS@Y-CQDs-6) exhibited the highest enhanced photocatalytic degradation of rhodamine B (RhB) with a degradation efficiency of 98% within 45 min, and the degradation rate constant was ~4.8 times higher than that of pristine CdS-NRs under visible light irradiation. The degradation of RhB is carried out through the formation of a green emissive intermediate N-deethylated RhB. The improved photocatalytic degradation performance of CdS@Y-CQDs nanocomposites is attributed to the formation of near-surface heterojunction of surface-adsorbed specific-shaped Y-CQDs, which can widen the range of light absorption and promote the separation of photogenerated electron-hole pairs in CdS-NRs by trapping and boosting the transfer of photo excited charge carriers over the surface.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic for the synthesis of specific shaped (quadrilateral and trilateral) yellow-emissive Y-CQDs and CdS@Y-CQDs heterostructure nanocomposite photocatalysts.
Schematic of the formation mechanism of specific-shaped (quadrilateral and trilateral) yellow-emissive Y-CQDs.

(a) HR-TEM image of Y-CQDs (white contour lines highlight the quadrilateral and trilateral projections); HR-TEM images of (b) CdS-NRs and (c) CdS@Y-CQDs nanocomposite; (d) AmperometricI-t curves at illumination of 100 mW∙cm−2 with different numbers of intermittent off–on irradiation cycles.

(a) XRD patterns of phloroglucinol (black line) and Y-CQDs (red line); (b) Comparative XRD patterns of Y-CQDs, CdS-NRs, and CdS@Y-CQDs nanocomposites; (c) magnified image of CdS@Y-CQDs XRD diffraction showing the presence Y-CQDs peak; (d) Comparative Raman spectra of Y-CQDs, CdS-NRs, and CdS@Y-CQDs nanocomposites.

(a) Comparative FT-IR spectra of Y-CQDs, CdS-NRs, and CdS@Y-CQDs nanocomposites; (b) XPS total survey spectra of Y-CQDs, CdS-NRs, and CdS@Y-CQDs-6 nanocomposite; (c) high-resolution Cd 3d XPS spectra of CdS-NRs and CdS@Y-CQDs-6; (d) high-resolution C1s XPS spectra of Y-CQDs and CdS@Y-CQDs-6; (e) high-resolution S2p XPS spectra of CdS-NRs and CdS@Y-CQDs-6; (f) high-resolution S2p XPS spectra of Y-CQDs; (g) comparative high-resolution S2p XPS spectra of CdS-NRs, Y-CQDs, and CdS@Y-CQDs-6.

(a) UV–vis absorption spectra (black line), PL excitation (red line), and PL emission spectra (blue line) of Y-CQDs; UV–vis absorption spectra (pink line) of phloroglucinol (b) Emission spectra of Y-CQDs at different excitation wavelengths (250–510 nm) (c) UV–vis DRS spectra of CdS-NRs and CdS@Y-CQDs nanocomposites.

Gradual decrease in absorption peak of RhB under solar light in the presence of (a) CdS-NRs, (b) CdS@Y-CQDs-6, and (c) Y-CQDs; the insets show the digital images of time-dependent discolorations of RhB after photodegradation. (d) Concentration decay profile (C/C0) of RhB as a function of time in the presence of CdS-NRs, Y-CQDs, and CdS@Y-CQDs nanocomposite photocatalysts. (e) Kinetics of photocatalytic degradation of RhB: plot of −ln(C/C0) vs. irradiation time for each photocatalyst. (f) Plot of reaction rate constants (κ) for different photocatalysts.
(a) Reusability of the CdS@Y-CQDs photocatalyst for photodegradation of RhB; (b) XRD patterns of CdS@Y-CQDs before and after the photocatalytic reaction.

(a) Transient photocurrent response curves of CdS-NRs, Y-CQDs, and CdS@Y-CQDs nanocomposite photocatalysts. RhB fluorescence spectra as a function of visible light irradiation time in the presence of (b) CdS-NRs, (c) CdS@Y-CQDs-6, and (d) Y-CQDs; the insets show the digital images of time-dependent change of RhB fluorescence followed by photodegradation.
Schematic of the mechanism of photocatalytic RhB degradation over the CdS@Y-CQDs nanocomposites under visible light irradiation.
Comparison data of state-of-the-art carbon-containing catalyst performance for RhB degradation.

head.ce:title: Shape-engineered carbon quantum dots embedded on CdS-nanorods for enhanced visible light harvesting towards photocatalytic application

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemical and Biological Engineering, Gachon University, 1342 Seongnam-daero, Seongnam-si, Gyeonggi-do 13120, Republic of Korea

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemical and Biological Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemical and Biological Engineering, Gachon University, 1342 Seongnam-daero, Seongnam-si, Gyeonggi-do 13120, Republic of Korea

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Shape-engineered yellow-emissive carbon quantum dots (Y-CQDs) with discernible edges were synthesized. Three-fold-symmetric benzene-1,3,5-triol was a suitable precursor for controlling the specific structure and shape of CQDs via dehydration-facilitated covalent polymerization in sulfuric acid within 90 min of a thermal reaction at 190 °C. The formation mechanism involves ring cyclization of benzene-1,3,5-triol via a six-membered fashion through elimination of the neighboring active -H and –OH groups. Binary heterostructure nanocomposites, comprising CdS-nanorods (NRs) and Y-CQDs, were prepared using simple sonication followed by solvent evaporation under stirring. The nanocomposite with 6 wt% YCQDs (CdS@Y-CQDs-6) exhibited the highest enhanced photocatalytic degradation of rhodamine B (RhB) with a degradation efficiency of 98% within 45 min, and the degradation rate constant was ~4.8 times higher than that of pristine CdS-NRs under visible light irradiation. The degradation of RhB is carried out through the formation of a green emissive intermediate N-deethylated RhB. The improved photocatalytic degradation performance of CdS@Y-CQDs nanocomposites is attributed to the formation of near-surface heterojunction of surface-adsorbed specific-shaped Y-CQDs, which can widen the range of light absorption and promote the separation of photogenerated electron-hole pairs in CdS-NRs by trapping and boosting the transfer of photo excited charge carriers over the surface.

ce:section.ce:para: Little improvement has been attained in the synthesis of specific-shaped CQDs with a discernible edge structure, compared to perovskite and semiconductor quantum dots. From a synthesis perspective, CQDs with graphitic crystalline nature can be simply obtained via a top-down approach, but the crystal uniformity, shape, and size are not preserved. Conversely, bottom-up approaches produce uniform-sized CQDs easily, but little improvement has been attained on engineering their specific shape [1–3]. The engineering of the specific shape of CQDs in bottom-up method suffers mostly from improper selection of the precursor and difficulty in monitoring reaction time. Nonetheless, Lee et al. engineered blue-emissive crystalline hexagonal GQDs based on catalytic solution chemistry using Amadori rearrangement [4]. Yuan et al. engineered multicolor triangular CQDs by a hydrothermal method that required a prolonged reaction time and sophisticated purification techniques (column chromatographic) for the separation [5]. Ye et al. synthesized regular-shaped size-dependent multi-color emissive GQDs [6]. Tang et al. reported size-tunable spherical-shaped CQDs of multi-color emission by adjusting the heating time in microwave [7,8]. Accordingly, attempts are in progress to synthesize CQDs with desired specific shapes via a facile and rapid synthesis method [9].

CdS is an outstanding visible-light-active photocatalyst due to its suitable band gap (2.4 eV), which is a prerequisite for dye degradation and hydrogen production [10–12]. Among the several morphologies of CdS-NRs, a one-dimensional nanostructure with very high length-to-diameter ratio has potential advantages over the others because of unidirectional transport of photogenerated electrons [13,14]. However, photoinduced charge carriers on the surface of CdS-NRs often recombine inherently, resulting in low performance during photocatalysis [15]. Consequently, several strategies have been employed to suppress the recombination of electron-hole pairs or decrease the self-photo-corrosion via surface modification of CdS-NRs with various metal nanoparticles, semiconductors, and carbon materials [16–18].

Of late, several strategies have been developed to anchor the surface of CdS-NRs homogeneously with conjugated carbon layers to form near-coaxial heterojunction [19–21]. Liang et al. embedded CdS-NRs with triptycene-based covalent polymers to widen visible light absorption range, enhance the trapping, transfer the photoexcited charge carriers, and decrease the photo-corrosion of CdS-NRs. [22]. Lei et al. reported graphene quantum dots coupled with CdS nanoparticles for enhanced photocatalytic H2 evolution, where the graphene-like-nature of GQDs makes them mainly act as effective electron acceptor to maintain the charge separation rather than as a photosensitizer [23]. Jiang et al. adopted a microwave-assisted solvothermal method using ionic-liquid precursors to synthesize a series of reduced graphene oxide–CdS nanocomposites with improved efficiency for photocatalytic degradation of RhB, and the enhancement ability was attributed to enhanced adsorbability, extended light response, and better separation of electron–hole pairs [24]. Hence, over the last decade, significant effort has been dedicated to enhancing visible light harvesting via anchoring the surface of semiconductors with carbon-based conjugated nanostructures (graphene quantum dots, porous covalent polymers, covalent organic frameworks), which can provide larger surface area, more active sites, and enhanced charge carriers transport ability [25–27].

In this study, quadrilateral and trilateral shaped CQDs were engineered via a wet-chemistry-based facile thermal heating process (190 °C) using benzene-1,3,5-triol in sulfuric acid. The as-synthesized specific-shaped yellow-emissive Y-CQDs were believed to be formed via dehydration-facilitated covalent polymerization and precise growth. The mechanism of formation was well elucidated using numerous characterization techniques. Y-CQDs were embedded on the surface of CdS-NRs, yielding a heterostructure photocatalyst, as illustrated in Fig. 1 . CdS@Y-CQDs nanocomposites exhibited a significant enhancement in photocatalytic RhB degradation under visible light irradiation. The CdS@Y-CQDs-6 photocatalyst exhibited the best photocatalytic performance with a degradation efficiency of 98% within 45 min and the degradation rate constant was ~4.8 times higher than that of pristine CdS-NRs. The improved photocatalytic degradation mechanism was explained thoroughly.

In a typical synthesis procedure, cadmium nitrate tetrahydrate (1.92 g) and thiourea (1.42 g) were dissolved in ethylenediamine (50 mL) and stirred for 10 min to obtain a homogeneous solution. The solution was then transferred to a Teflon-lined autoclave (100-mL) and placed in a hot air oven at 180 °C for 24 h. After cooling naturally to room temperature (RT) the yellow solution was centrifuged at 8000 rpm for 10 min and washed with ethanol and deionized water alternatively several times. Finally, the solution was dried at 60 °C overnight to get the yellow powder of CdS-NRs as displayed in Fig. S1a (electronic supplementary information (ESI†)).

Specific-shaped (quadrilateral and trilateral) CQDs were synthesized according to our previous reports [28]. A wet chemistry based facile thermal heating process was adopted without the use of any expensive and sophisticated equipment. 2 mL of water was placed in a beaker followed by the addition of 200 mg of benzene-1,3,5-triol [Fig. S1b (ESI†)] and 2 mL of concentrated sulfuric acid. The clear pale yellowish-colored solution was put in a preheated hot air oven at constant 190 °C. After 90 min of thermal heating the beaker was removed from the oven and naturally cooled to ambient temperature. DI water (5 mL) was added to the black colored reaction product. Then, high speed centrifugation (12900 rpm) was performed to get the precipitate of Y-CQDs. After washing the precipitate with water, dialysis was performed against DI water for 48 h using a cellulose ester membrane (mol. wt. cut-off: 500 Da). The dialyzed dispersion was dried and dispersed again in absolute ethanol followed by centrifugation for 5 min at 6000 rpm to discard the larger particles. Finally, black colored powder of Y-CQDs [Fig. S1c (ESI†)] were obtained after drying the filtrate.

CdS@Y-CQDs (1D@0D) binary heterojunction composite was prepared using simple sonication followed by a solvent evaporation technique while stirring as illustrated in Fig. 1. First, 100 mg of CdS-NRs and different amounts of Y-CQDs (2, 4, 6, and 8 wt%) were dispersed in 30 mL of absolute ethanol in a 100-mL glass beaker. Then the mixtures were sonicated for 15 min and the solvent was evaporated overnight at 60 °C while stirring. The obtained nanocomposite mixtures were denoted as CdS@Y-CQDs-2, CdS@Y-CQDs-4, CdS@Y-CQDs-6, and CdS@Y-CQDs-8, respectively.

RhB dye was chosen as the test organic pollutant and the photocatalytic efficiency of the photocatalyst was investigated using the degradation of RhB under visible light. All the photodegradation experiments were carried out at ambient temperature using 10 mg of photocatalyst in 100 mL of RhB (10 ppm). During the photo-degradation experiments, the source of visible light was a solar simulator 100 W Xe lamp. The reaction temperature was maintained constant using a water circulation regulator. The catalyst was homogeneously dispersed by stirring at 250 rpm. To ensure the attainment of adsorption–desorption equilibrium between the photocatalyst and the dye mixture, the solution was kept in the dark while stirring for 30 min, prior to illumination with light. 4.0 mL of the sample solution was pipetted out and centrifuged after certain time intervals. Ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) absorption spectra of the photo-degraded sample was recorded at 552 nm to monitor the concentration during the photoreaction.

A three-fold symmetric poly-phenolic molecule (benzene-1,3,5-triol, a triangulogen) with distinctive structure and appropriate symmetry (C3h, symmetry elements: E, C3, C3 2, σh, S3, S3 -1) plays an important role in engineering the shape and structure of CQDs via dehydration facilitated covalent polymerization in sulfuric acid medium [5]. Herein, specific-shaped (quadrilateral and trilateral) yellow-emissive Y-CQDs were prepared through elimination of the neighboring active -H and –OH groups via ring cyclization of a single precursor (benzene-1,3,5-triol) via a six-membered fashion in sulfuric acid through a simple thermal heating process. The molecular symmetry and distinct structure of the precursor possesses advantages in terms of the formation of such specific-shaped Y-CQDs [Fig. S2 ((ESI†)]. The plausible mechanism of Y-CQDs formation is elucidated in Fig. 2 . 1H NMR (methanol‑d 4, ppm) and 13C NMR (methanol‑d 4, ppm) spectral data of the precursor (benzene-1,3,5-triol) and Y-CQDs are detailed in Fig. S3 (ESI†). Comparing different proton (1H NMR) and carbon (13C NMR) environments between Y-CQDs and benzene-1,3,5-triol, it was confirmed that a number of aromatic rings were fused to construct a large polyaromatic Y-CQDs. The 1H NMR spectra of Y-CQDs (Fig. S3b (ESI†)) contain several emerged peaks compared to the precursor [Fig. S3a (ESI†)] at approximately 7.3–7.6 ppm, which analogous to obvious polyaromatic proton peaks signifying several aromatic rings were fused to form large polyaromatic Y-CQDs. Y-CQDs also exhibited various new peaks at approximately 6.5–6.9 ppm, corresponding to active –OH (hydroxyl) protons and -SH (thiol)/-SO3H (sulfonic acid) protons. Furthermore, 13C NMR spectra [Fig. S3d (ESI†)] of Y-CQDs was comprised of several emerged resonance signals in the range 145–166 ppm corresponding to the conjugated carbon (sp2 hybridized) atoms bonded to –OH (hydroxyl) groups at the edge. In addition, there were several signals identified at approximately 115–140 ppm in the 13C NMR spectra compared to the precursor (benzene-1,3,5-triol), evidencing the construction of intact sp2 domains.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structural and morphological characterization of Y-CQDs and CdS@Y-CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: In-depth characterizations were performed to reveal insights into the structure and shape of the yellow emissive Y-CQDs. Fig. 3 a displays the high-resolution transmission electron microscope (HR-TEM) image of Y-CQDs with narrow size distribution and distinguishable unique crystalline quadrilateral /trilateral structure with size distribution of approximately 5–8 nm. It is difficult to state the percentage of quadrilateral and trilateral exactly among all the Y-CQDs, however from the HR-TEM image it can be roughly estimated to be 1:1 in ratio. The HR-TEM image exhibits a distinct lattice fringe of 0.21 nm, corresponding to the inter-planar separation of the (100) plane and indicating crystalline graphene structure [5]. Moreover, a photocurrent investigation was conducted using a three-electrode system to examine the quantum confinement effect-induced intrinsic transition activities of the quantum-sized Y-CQDs. The photocurrent measurement experiment was performed in a Na2SO4 (0.5 M) electrolyte using Ag/AgCl as the reference electrode and a platinum wire as counter electrode. Y-CQDs dispersion (2 mg/mL in ethanol) was drop-casted on ITO glass and used as a working electrode. The transitory photocurrent signal over alternating on–off visible-light irradiation cycles was recorded (Fig. 3d). Upon photo-irradiation on Y-CQDs, an average photocurrent of 0.2 μA/cm2 was produced, implying the generation of electron-hole pairs separation, which in turn indicates that Y-CQDs has semiconductor-like behavior [29]. On the other hand, Fig. S4 (ESI†) and Fig. 3b show the TEM and HR-TEM images of pristine CdS-NRs, respectively, with clean and smooth surfaces and nothing adhere to it. The HR-TEM image (Fig. 3b) shows the crystalline lattice fringe with lattice spacing of 0.334 nm which is assigned to the (002) plane of CdS-NRs [30]. HR-TEM image of CdS@Y-CQDs (Fig. 3c) show that a narrow transparent layer of Y-CQDs was successfully embedded on the outer surface of CdS-NRs and the rod structure was well retained. The close contact of Y-CQDs layer on the CdS-NRs provide more active sites and assist the interfacial charge transfer, which helps in enhancing the visible light harvest towards photocatalytic degradation process. The SEM image (Fig. S5a ((ESI†)) of the pristine CdS-NRs show uniform nanorods having lengths of approximately 1–1.5 μm and an average diameter of approximately 50–55 nm, while after modification with Y-CQDs (Fig. S5b ((ESI†)), the average diameter drastically changes to 60–65 nm suggesting a successful adherence of Y-CQDs on the surface of CdS-NRs. The EDS mapping of the CdS@Y-CQDs as shown in Figs. S6 (ESI†) confirms the presence of C (green), O (red), Cd (yellow), and S(pink), indicating that the Y-CQDs layer was homogeneously embedded on the surface of the CdS-NRs. The nano-sized Y-CQDs endowed them a high surface free energy because of the strong bonding between the Y-CQDs and CdS-NRs. To further confirm the successful formation of the CdS@Y-CQDs composite, 1H NMR spectra of pristine Y-CQDs and CdS@Y-CQDs composite were recorded as shown in Fig. S7 (ESI†). After the construction of the CdS@Y-CQDs composite, similar peaks were noticed at approximately 6.5–6.9 ppm (corresponding to protons of the edge functional groups) and 7.3– 7.6 ppm (analogous to aromatic protons signals) that were also present in the pure Y-CQDs.

X-ray diffraction (XRD) arrays of benzene-1,3,5-triol and the Y-CQDs, are depicted in Fig. 4 a. Highly crystalline benzene-1,3,5-triol exhibits peaks at 16.8°, 21.6°, 22.9°, 25.2°, and 27.3°, which are entirely converted to an obvious broad peak at approximately 22.5°, which is attributed to the (001) plane of a graphene-like structure with crystalline nature [31,32]. Another shoulder peak at approximately 10° may be ascribed to the enhanced surface air-oxidation in the presence of aerobic O2 during the dehydration facilitated controlled growth process [33]. The XRD peak is in good accordance with the TEM results. Thus, the XRD result indicates the construction of Y-CQDs via dehydration facilitated covalent polymerization and graphitization. Fig. 4b shows the combined XRD peak of the Y-CQDs, pristine CdS-NRs, and CdS@Y-CQDs nanocomposite. Pristine CdS-NRs shows diffraction peaks at 24.6°, 26.3°, 28°, 36.4°, 43.5°, 47.7°, 51.7°, 66.7°, and 69° corresponding to the (100), (002), (101), (102), (110), (103), (112), (203), and (210) planes, respectively, which is in good agreement with the hexagonal phase (JCPDS 01-077-2306) [22,34]. As shown in Fig. 4b the XRD peak of the Y-CQDs were less prominent in the CdS@Y-CQDs nanocomposites compared to the CdS-NRs XRD peaks because of its lower percentage, less intensity, and broadness. Fig. 4c is the close-up image of CdS@Y-CQDs XRD diffraction showing the presence of a peak at approximately 22.5°, corresponding to the (001) plane of the Y-CQDs. Interestingly, the diffraction patterns of the CdS-NRs and CdS@Y-CQDs show similar peak positions without any shifting, suggesting that the Y-CQDs layer has no effect on the crystallinity of the CdS-NRs.

The intrinsic structural property of Y-CQDs displayed high degree of graphitization at approximately 1200–1800 cm−1 in the Raman spectra (Fig. 4d). As shown in Fig. 4d, the usual peak observed at approximately 1590 cm−1 signifies the G-band, demonstrating the sp2 hybridized carbon networks. The D-band peak is observed at approximately 1340 cm−1, indicative of the defective or disordered carbon framework produced due to the aerial-oxidation of surface. I G/I D (G to D peak intensity ratio) value is more than unity, suggesting the construction of CQDs structure with high quality graphene, which is in good accordance with the HR-TEM results. The Raman spectra of pristine CdS-NRs display two significant characteristic peaks positioned at approximately 301 and 602 cm−1, which may be attributed to the first and second order longitudinal optical modes of hexagonal CdS, respectively [34]. On the other hand, Raman spectra of CdS@Y-CQDs show all the Raman bands for CdS-NRs and Y-CQDs with a gradual increase in Y-CQDs bands, implying the successful formation of a strong CdS@Y-CQDs heterostructure. The intensity of bands at 301 and 602 cm−1 in CdS@Y-CQDs decreases compared to pristine CdS-NRs, indicating the successful attachment of Y-CQDs to CdS-NRs, which in turn minimize the recombination rate of electron-hole pairs and enhances the photocatalytic activity.

To inspect the surface morphology, functional groups, and chemical composition of all the samples, X-ray photoelectron (XPS) and Fourier-transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were recorded. The FT-IR spectral record (Fig. 5 a) of the pristine Y-CQDs exhibits characteristic stretching vibration peaks at approximately 3340 and 1170 cm−1 corresponding to C-S/C-O and O-H, respectively [35]. The stretching vibration at approximately 1590 cm−1 may be ascribed to CC of polyaromatic carbon framework system with phenolic hydroxyl groups located at the edges [36,37]. Two emerged peaks at approximately 1028 and 1360 cm−1 are attributed to -SO3H stretching vibration, indicating that the surface contains -SO3H groups together with hydroxyl groups [38]. FT-IR spectrum of CdS-NRs contains the characteristic peak of Cd-S stretching at approximately 621 cm−1 [39]. As expected, all the characteristic peaks of CdS-NRs and Y-CQDs appear in the FT-IR spectra of the CdS@Y-CQDs heterostructure.

XPS was analyzed to inspect the elemental composition and their valence state in the CdS-NRs, Y-CQDs, and CdS@Y-CQDs nanocomposites. The XPS total survey spectra (Fig. 5b) of the Y-CQDs contain mainly C, O, and S (three elements) while that of CdS@Y-CQDs is composed of Cd, C, O, and S (four elements), as expected from the FT-IR analysis. The high resolution XPS spectrum (Fig. 5c) for Cd 3d of the pristine CdS-NRs displays two peaks at 404.58 and 411.28 eV, which correspond to 3d5/2 (Cd2+) and 3d3/2 (Cd2+), respectively [22]. It is to be noted that both the binding energies of 3d3/2 shift toward the higher energy at 404.88 and 411.68 eV in CdS@Y-CQDs, which demonstrate strong electronegative interaction between CdS-NRs and Y-CQDs [40]. The de-convoluted C1s XPS spectra of Y-CQDs (Fig. 5d), exhibited three peaks positioned at 284.9, 286.8, and 289 eV corresponding to CC, C-S, and C-O, respectively [35]. Whereas, that of CdS@Y-CQDs shows similar three peaks of CC, C-S, and C-O with values slightly shifted to 284.8, 286.4, and 288.7 eV, respectively. The shifting of binding energies (C-O and C-S) in the composite compared to pristine Y-CQDs, further demonstrates that surface active functional groups help in strong intimate contact rather than simple physical mixture [22]. Fig. 5e is the de-convoluted S2p high-resolution XPS analysis of CdS-NRs, displaying two peaks at 161 and 162.3 eV corresponding to S 2p3/2 and S 2p1/2, respectively, indicative of the existence of S2- state. On the other hand, that of the CdS@Y-CQDs composite shows S2p3/2 and S2p1/2 peaks with slight shifted values at 161.3 and 162.6 eV, respectively, similar to the shift of Cd peak, indicating strong interaction between CdS-NRs and Y-CQDs. The de-convoluted S2p XPS analysis of Y-CQDs (Fig. 5f) displays two peaks at 164.4 and 169 eV which corresponds to -SH and -SO3H, respectively. [35,41–44]. The results specifically indicate that the sulfonation of polyphenolic-polyaromatic Y-CQDs occurred at high temperatures. Therefore, the de-convoluted XPS analysis of S2p and C1s indicated that Y-CQDs are composed of C-O, CC, C-S, -SH, and -C-SO3H chemical bonds. Fig. 5g shows that the CdS@Y-CQDs composite contains both types of S2p peak, originating from CdS-NRs and Y-CQDs. Therefore, it is further confirmed that the Y-CQDs were strongly intimated on the surface of CdS-NRs.

To inspect the optical properties of all the samples, UV–vis absorption spectra, photoluminescence (PL) spectra, and diffuse reflectance spectra (DRS) were analyzed. The Y-CQDs were more dispersible in alcohols than in water. Fig. 6 a is the combined spectra of UV–vis absorption, PL excitation, and PL emission of the Y-CQDs dispersion in ethanol. Benzene-1,3,5-triol exhibited a typical sharp absorption peak at approximately 266 nm, corresponding to the π–π* transition of a polyphenolic ring [45] . Y-CQDs exhibited an obvious bathochromic shifted characteristic π–π* (Csp2 = Csp2) transition band at 305 nm compared to benzene-1,3,5-triol (266 nm), due to the construction of a lower energy conjugated aromatic carbogenic core (Csp2 = Csp2) hybridized with surface functional groups [46,47]. The PL excitation spectra of the Y-CQDs show two excitation peaks having maxima at λex = 370 nm and the PL emission spectra exhibit maximum emission wavelength at λem = 550 nm corresponding to yellow emission. Additionally, the Y-CQDs were excited at various excitation wavelength ranging from 250 to 510 nm and PL emission spectra were examined, which exhibit the excitation-dependent-emission nature as shown in Fig. 6b. Furthermore, DRS of the pristine CdS-NRs and all the CdS@Y-CQDs nanocomposites are presented in Fig. 6c, which shows the excellent photo-absorption property of all the samples from UV light to visible light. Pristine CdS-NRs exhibited sharp photo-absorption edge at 520 nm, which is due to the intrinsic band gap absorption [48]. As the amount of Y-CQDs loading increases on the surface, the visible light-absorption property widened, in addition to the band gap absorption of CdS. As Y-CQDs mass ratio is increased on the surface of CdS-NRs, the optical absorption can be significantly widened from the 520–800 nm range resulting in improved photocatalytic performances for all the CdS@Y-CQDs nanocomposites compared to CdS-NRs [48,49]. More importantly, after the coating of Y-CQDs on the CdS-NRs surface, there were almost no differences in the inherent band structure of the CdS-NRs, indicating that Y-CQDs was coated on the surface, rather than located inside the crystal grating of the CdS-NRs, as predicted using XRD [48].

The photocatalytic performance of all the synthesized nanocomposites was estimated via photo-degradation of RhB as the target molecule under visible light. Fig. 7 (a–c) show the gradual decrease in intensity of characteristic absorption peak of RhB at 554 nm under visible light in the presence of CdS-NRs, CdS@Y-CQDs-6, and Y-CQDs, respectively. Figs. S8 (a) and (b) (ESI†) show the gradual attenuation of absorption intensity of RhB under visible light in the presence of CdS@Y-CQDs-4 and CdS@Y-CQDs-8 nanocomposite, respectively. The insets show the digital image of time-dependent discolorations of RhB after photodegradation. Fig. 7d is the concentration decay profile (C/C0) of RhB as a function of time in the presence of the CdS-NRs, CdS@Y-CQDs-4, CdS@Y-CQDs-6, and CdS@Y-CQDs-8 photocatalysts. A blank experiment was performed to check the self-decomposition of RhB under visible light and the result showed almost negligible degradation during 45 min of irradiation, while 55% degradation took place in the presence of the pristine CdS-NRs. Furthermore, the photocatalytic performance of all the CdS@Y-CQDs nanocomposites was evaluated, which shows higher degradation rate compared to the pristine CdS-NRs. CdS@Y-CQDs-6 shows the best photocatalytic performance with a degradation efficiency of 98% within 45 min, while CdS@Y-CQDs-4 and CdS@Y-CQDs-8 exhibit 93% and 94%, respectively, under the same light irradiation conditions. The Y-CQDs were also tested, but they show very weak photocatalytic performances. They exhibited 57% adsorption before irradiation, probably due to the strong electrostatic interaction between negatively charged hydroxyl or sulphonyl group and positively charged RhB dye. Fig. 7e shows the plot of -ln(C/C0) vs time for better understanding of the kinetics of degradation. The degradation reaction of RhB follows pseudo-first-order kinetics and the rates were calculated to be 0.018, 0.064, 0.087, and 0.067 min−1 for CdS-NRs, CdS@Y-CQDs-4, CdS@Y-CQDs-6, and CdS@Y-CQDs-8 nanocomposites, respectively (Fig. 7f). The Y-CQDs significantly enhance the activity of the CdS-NRs towards the photo-degradation of RhB dye, and the CdS@Y-CQDs-6 nanocomposite shows approximately 4.8 times superior efficiency compared to CdS-NRs.

we have performed the cycling performance experiment up to 3 cycles to confirm the nanocomposite stability and to investigate the phase stability, XRD spectra of the nanocomposite was recorded before and after the cycling performance. To get the sample in bulk amount for XRD after the cycling performance, we have taken 50 mg catalyst and for each cycle photodegradation reaction was conducted for 35 min under visible light for each cycle. During the photocatalysis, all the other experimental parameters were kept unaltered. Fig. 8 a exhibits the reusability of CdS@CQDs nanocomposite for the degradation of RhB over three cycles. The photocatalyst was separated through centrifugation and washed after each photocatalytic experiment. The washed catalyst was used again for the degradation of a freshly prepared RhB dye solution (10 ppm). Even after the cycling performance experiment up to 3 cycles, there was negligible change in photocatalytic activity of CdS@CQDs nanocomposite. Furthermore, comparative investigation of XRD pattern (Fig. 8b) before and after recycling experiments, reveals that crystalline phase and structure of CdS@CQDs (before and after the cycling experiment) exactly coincided. This confirms that even after the recycling experiments up to three cycles the crystalline phase of the photocatalyst was stable.

There have been several reports on the modification of CdS with conjugated carbon-based nanostructure for enhanced visible light harvesting toward photocatalytic dye degradation. But to the best of our knowledge CQDs have not been utilized with CdS nanorods for the degradation of RhB. CQDs @CdS microsphere was utilized earlier for the degradation purpose of RhB but the photocatalytic performance was poor compared to the present CdS@CQDs system [50]. The comparison data for photocatalytic performance of CQDs modified CdS has been shown in Table S1 (ESI†). Apart from CQDs, several carbon-based conjugated nanostructures have been used to modify CdS for degradation of RhB. Table 1 below shows the comparison data of state-of-the-art carbon-containing catalyst performance for RhB degradation, indicating the superiority of our system over others [51–63].

It is well proved that active radicals ( • OH, O2
•
−, and h+) play a crucial role in photocatalysis. CdS, with a band gap of 2.4 eV, is a visible light active photocatalyst and its valence band (VB) electrons can be photoexcited to the conductance band (CB) easily through exposure to visible light. The Y-CQDs, having high carrier mobility, can accept and transport the photogenerated electrons efficiently, hindering the recombination process through electron-hole pair separation. Fig. 9 a shows the electrochemical investigation of the photoinduced charge separation efficiency of the CdS-NRs, Y-CQDs, and CdS@Y-CQDs composites. The transitory photocurrent signals over alternating on–off visible-light irradiation cycles were recorded on a three-electrode system. All the CdS@Y-CQDs nanocomposites exhibited improved photocurrent densities compared to pristine CdS-NRs. The pristine CdS-NRs exhibited low average photocurrent generation (0.01 mA/cm2) due to the higher rate of electron-hole pair recombination. All the CdS@Y-CQDs nanocomposites show higher average photocurrent densities compared to the bare CdS-NRs, with CdS@Y-CQDs-6 showing the highest value, which is approximately 3.2 times higher.

During the course of the photodegradation of RhB over CdS@Y-CQDs nanocomposites, an interesting observation is the progressive decrease of the absorption maxima with a hypsochromic shift, as shown in Fig. 7b. RhB exhibits a characteristic absorption maxima at 554 nm and there was no spectral shift witnessed during the dark reaction for 30 min. Fig. 7a exhibits gradual attenuation of the absorption maxima along with a very slight hypsochromic shift in presence of the bare CdS-NRs during photo-treatment. After 45 min of irradiation, the peak appears at 551 nm. In contrast, CdS@Y-CQDs nanocomposites exhibit rapid attenuation of the absorption peak of RhB at 554 nm and the characteristic absorbance maxima shifted to 500 nm gradually during photodegradation reaction. It is noteworthy that the sharp pink color rapidly changes to greenish yellow before finally becomes colorless. Thus, the CdS@Y-CQDs photocatalyst showed a pronounced hypsochromic shift of absorption spectra compared to pristine CdS-NRs. The hypsochromic shifts might result from the formation of several N-deethylated-RhB intermediates and stepwise fragmentation of conjugated chromophore-structure [64–67]. However, Y-CQDs showed a bathochromic shift (λmax = 564 nm) along with the attenuation of absorption maxima (Fig. 7c), probably due to the π-π stacking between the carbogenic aromatic core of Y-CQDs and conjugated chromophore of RhB [68]. To better understand the photodegradation mechanism in support with the spectral shift of absorption maxima, PL spectra of photo-degraded RhB were recorded for all the samples. Fig. 9 (b–d) show the PL emission spectra of the photo-degraded RhB collected at different time intervals during photo-irradiation in the presence of the CdS-NRs, CdS@Y-CQDs-6 nanocomposite, and Y-CQDs, respectively. As shown in Fig. 9b, the bare CdS-NRs exhibit gradual attenuation of PL emission maxima along with a very slight hypsochromic shift during 75 min of photo-treatment. Similar to the UV–vis absorption spectra, PL spectra also exhibited a gradual hypsochromic shift for all the nanocomposites and the fluorescence rapidly changed from orange to green due to the formation of new intermediates. Fig. 9c shows the progressive evolution of the new PL emission maxima corresponding to green emissive intermediates and its photodecomposition during light irradiation in the presence of CdS@Y-CQDs-6. Fig. S9 (a–b) (ESI†) shows the progressive evolution of the similar PL emission maxima corresponding to green emissive intermediates in the presence of CdS@Y-CQDs-4 and CdS@Y-CQDs-8, respectively. Cinelli et al. investigated similar photo-degraded product by monitoring PL data at different excitation wavelengths (554 nm, 530 nm, 510 nm and 495 nm) and confirmed the presence of at least four different kinds of N-deethylated-RhB intermediates [64]. Similarly, our results are in accordance with several reported studies and support the finding that the degradation process occurs through the N-deethylated-RhB intermediates. Moreover, the PL emission spectra of the photodegradation products in the presence of the pristine Y-CQDs were also tested as shown in Fig. 9d. The figure shows strong fluorescence quenching of RhB dye using Y-CQDs along with bathochromic shift of PL emission maxima, probably due to the π-π stacking between the carbogenic aromatic core of Y-CQDs and conjugated chromophore of RhB.

Furthermore, radical scavenging study was performed to explain the photocatalytic mechanism as shown in Fig S10 (ESI†). Photocatalytic reaction usually produces reactive radical species which includes • OH, O2
•
−, and h+. Distinct scavengers can therefore be used to differentiate the vital roles of active radicalspecies in the photoreaction under visiblelight [69–74]. Benzoquinone (BQ, 1 mM), disodium ethylenediamine tetraacetic acid (EDTA-2Na, 1 mM), and isopropyl alcohol (IPA, 1 mM) were used as scavengers for O2
•
−, photogenerated h+, and • OH radical respectively. The maximum photodegradation of RhB over CdS@CQDs-6 was 98% (Fig. 7b). As shown in Fig S10a (ESI†), upon adding EDTA and IPA to the RhB reaction mixture separately, the photodegradation efficiency was reduced by only 5.5% and 5.4%, respectively. This suggests that • OH or h+ has very minor effect on the photodegradation process. But when BQ was added the photodegradation process was greatly suppressed by 53% (98% to 45%), suggesting O2
•
− radical was the major oxidative species responsible for photodegradation. The most interesting fact is that upon addition of BQ, photodegradation process does not undergo formation of N-de-ethylated RhB as evident from the unshifting of UV–vis spectra towards shorter wavelength and no change in fluorescence after degradation reaction (Fig S10b (ESI†)). Thus it can be concluded that the photoinduced electrons on CdS@CQDs heterostructure can easily reduce O2 to O2
•
− whereas the • OH formation by photogenerated h+ is less prominent. Thus O2
•
− was the major contributor in RhB photocatalytic degradation by CdS@CQDs.

Based on above results, a well-established mechanism for the photo-degradation process can be drawn (Fig. 10 ). The N-de-ethylation formation and photo-bleaching compete in the initial steps of the photoreaction. From the radical scavenging study, it was well understood that O2
•
− was the major contributor which controls the formation of N-de-ethylated RhB in the initial step of the photoreaction and formation of N-de-ethylated RhB is the primary rate determining step. The surface of the CdS@Y-CQDs nanocomposites are expected to be negatively charged due to the presence of hydroxyl and sulphonyl groups and this could be the probable reason for the strong electrostatic interaction or adsorption of RhB on the surface followed by photo-decomposition. Considering the oxidation potential of RhB and conduction band potential of CdS-NRs, it is evident that the photo-excited dye (RhB*) can transfer the photo-induced electron to the CB of the CdS-NRs. Furthermore, electrons are directly photoexcited to CB of the CdS-NRs on visible light irradiation. The major role of the Y-CQDs is to capture and transport these electrons to hinder the recombination process through electron-hole pair separation. The photogenerated negatively charged electrons can now react with dissolved oxygen to generate active superoxide radicals (O2 •−). Concurrently, holes react with H2O and generate hydroxyl radicals (•OH). Finally, the photogenerated active species (•OH, O2 •−, and h + ) with high oxidizing ability degrade RhB molecule into some small molecular entity or CO2 and H2O. Based on the results, the probable photo-degradation mechanism can be expressed as follows.
RhB + hν → RhB*
RhB* + CdS → RhB •+ + CdS (e-)
CdS + hν → CdS (e - + h+)
CdS (e-) + Y-CQDs → Y- CQDs (e-)
e- (CB, Y-CQDs) + O2 → O2 •−
OH¯ + h+ → •OH
RhB /RhB•+ + (h+, O2 •−, •OH) → Degraded products

In this work, an economical, rapid, wet chemistry-based method was adopted to prepare specific-shaped (quadrilateral and trilateral) Y-CQDs using benzene-1,3,5-triol as the precursor via a thermal heating method at 190 °C in a sulfuric acid medium. As-synthesized, specific-shaped (trilateral and quadrilateral) yellow emissive Y-CQDs were believed to be formed via dehydration facilitated covalent polymerization and controlled growth. The mechanism of formation was well elucidated with the help of numerous characterization techniques. Y-CQDs were embedded on the surface of CdS-NRs, yielding a heterostructure photocatalyst. The CdS@Y-CQDs nanocomposites exhibited the significant enhancement of photocatalytic RhB degradation under visible light irradiation. The CdS@Y-CQDs-6 photocatalyst exhibited the best photocatalytic performance with a degradation efficiency of 98% within 45 min and the degradation rate constant was approximately 4.8 times higher compared to that of pristine CdS-NRs. The degradation of RhB is carried out through the formation of a green emissive intermediate N-deethylated RhB. The improved visible light photocatalytic degradation performance of the CdS@Y-CQDs nanocomposites is attributed to the formation of near-surface heterojunction of surface-adsorbed, specific-shaped Y-CQDs, which can widen light absorption range, and promote the separation of photo generated electron-hole pairs in CdS-NRs through trapping and boosting the transfer of photo excited charge carriers over the surface.
